title: Break Free
notes: Break Free was one of a handful of songs that sprung from early 2006 studio sessions with Mark Batson.  It debuted on 6.13.06 in Toronto during a stretch of shows that also saw debuts of The Idea of You, Kill the King and Shotgun. Its lyrics are in the same vein as Hunger For the Great Light, with thinly-veiled erotic themes sung over a sleazy baritone guitar riff and Boyd's electric mandolin.The original structure of the song contained a main portion that included verses and choruses followed by an extended loose jam featuring a reserved sax solo by LeRoi, then a trumpet solo by Rashawn that increased the intensity to a climactic ending. It was played this way only three times.  On 6.21.06, it was played with a tightened structure that eliminated the final verse and LeRoi's solo.  This edit reduced the song by a minute or so and accelerated the slow, patient build found in earlier versions.  It stayed strong throughout the 2006 Summer Tour, mostly rotating nights with Idea of You.  The two songs were played a combined 47 times on the tour after Break Free's debut, but only at the same show 5 times.After the 2006 Summer Tour, it hadn't been played or mentioned again until it was played on the second night of the 2011 Atlantic City Caravan 6.25.11.  The song was played three times in four+ stops giving hope that it would make a full come back.  While there was no studio version officially released until 2023, a studio recording captured via IEM played at a soundcheck through the soundboard made its way around long before then. It is obviously a rough cut, with hardly final editing done. It contains backing vocals and overdubs, but no jam after the proper song concludes.The song has two live releases, the first on Live Trax 6 from 7.8.06 and a version featuring future traveling band member Jeff Coffin on sax from 8.15.06 that was released on the Warehouse 5 vol. 8 fan club disc.  There is no official release of a version with the original structure yet.
composer: Beauford / Lessard / Matthews / Moore / Tinsley / Batson

title: Ants Marching
notes: As Dave Matthews once put it, "This song is our anthem". It's perhaps one of the best known DMB songs and the all time most played set closer in history by a longshot. It incorporates every aspect of the band's personalities and bridges the DMB of old with the new. During its first years, Ants Marching was an instant favorite. It was usually preceeded by the 2001 Intro and was heavy on the keyboards. After Peter Greisar left the band, the intro evolved into a prelude build up similar to "Dueling Banjos". As the song progressed through 1993, it evolved its most recognizable feature: the Carter snare intro, which has sometimes lasted as long as a minute and a half. 1995 was the golden year of Ants. Not only was it the most played set closer, but it was also the most played song. It was during this time that Dave began letting the crowd sing "People in every direction". Further through the years, the song evolved slightly more to include a 1-2-3 round robin jam towards the end and has also featured guests on special occasions. According to calculations based on the number of times played and number of shows performed, any given show has about a 50% chance of containing Ants. On our site, snare intro in the notes denotes the Carter snare intro, direct intro denotes the quick the band intro.Starting in 2007, Rashawn began interpolating The Jacksons' "Shake Your Body (Down to the Ground)" after the second chorus in nearly every version of the song.

title: Warehouse
notes: This song ranks near the top of the list in terms of all time fan favorites. It was played since the very beginning and has remained a highlight nearly every show it is played. In the early days of 92-93, the song frequently featured a "Shortnin' Bread" interpolation towards the end of the song. After the summer of 1994, the last verse evolved beyond the mere repetition of "That's my blood down there". Starting in 1995, a stop-time intro began to occasionally be played during full-band versions. By 1996, the song was consistently being played with a stop-time intro. In 1999, fans from the original nancies mailing list began organizing to chant "Woo!" during the stop-time intro. By the summer of 2000, the "Woos" caught on and are now a standard crowd chant during every stop-time intro to Warehouse.Dave and Tim versions have continued to contain the original intro, as well as a "Passion" intro where Dave interpolates the vocals from Peter Gabriel's song "Passion." Beginning on 9.14.03, the jam evolved into what has come to be known as the 'salsa jam' outro with a more celebratory 'salsa' vibe. In 2007, the band began playing what we call an "improv intro." Since the chords Dave played during this intro are the same ones he plays during the Passion intro, some have also labeled this intro as "Passion"; however, this intro is completely unrelated to the Peter Gabriel song and is therefore distinct from the Passion intro. In 2010, the band began playing the intro exactly as it appears on the album.This song has also been featured as a fake, in which the Warehouse intro is played several times before the band goes directly into Ants Marching. Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitar

title: Two Step
notes: Two step is undoubtedly one of the founding pillars of live DMB songs. Since the very beginning, the song was established with a long extended celebratory jam. The early jams featured Peter Greisar's keyboards, Carter's drums, and Dave's salsa scatting. During 93-95, the song lost the salsa vibe and developed a more robust intro and outro. In late 1995, during the studio sessions, the band reworked the song to end on the same minor key with which it begins. This started a gradual extension of the song outro that would evolve for years to come and would feature a extensive solo from Carter. In terms of slotting, the song has proven worthy in all slots: opener, midset, closer, encore or 2nd encore. 1996 features by far the most performances played during any given year with the song being played a nearly every show. 2020 features the least amount with only 2 performances during the year. 9.19.2000 features the longest version ever clocking in at a whopping 27 minutes 34 seconds.Lyrically, the song began featuring the famous alternating lyrical intro in the summer of 1996. The themes used in this intro have evolved over time and have even been used in new songs. Transcriptions of the intros are listed in alternate lyrics.  Another lyrical note is Dave's frequent mention of "Helena" (in place of 'my love' in the official lyrics). This is widely thought to be a reference to Helen of Troy, one of the more famously courted women/godesses in western history-mythology, in support of the theory of the song being framed in a courtship.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

notes: This song was originally known as "41 Police," a name derived from a combination of its being the forty-first song Dave wrote and its sounding like a Police song. "Police" was most likely not intended to be a final name for the song but rather a way of identifying it from other songs when it was new (see also "40 Always").After being perfected during the studio recording sessions of Crash, #41 came to be the most played song of 1996 and an instant classic. When Béla Fleck and the Flecktones guested on the song on NYE 1996, a marriage was made between #41 and the Flecktones song "Sojourn of Arjuna". Since then #41-Sojourn has become a tradition between DMB and the Flecktones whenever they play together. The Flecktones have even played a bit of #41 during their own performances of Sojourn of Arjuna.#41 has been played every year since its debut and has been a regular feature of both Dave Matthews Band and Dave & Tim setlists, with the exception of 2006, when it was a relative rarity.Interpolations:Caprice No. 24 - Niccolò Paganini"Common Free Style" - The RH Factor (interpolated in nearly every performance since June 2014)"Everyday" - Dave Matthews Band"Fantasy" - Earth, Wind & Fire"Getaway" - Earth, Wind & Fire"Girl from Ipanema" - Astrud Gilberto"If You Want Me to Stay" - Sly & the Family Stone"Jeannie" (theme from I Dream of Jeannie)"My Favorite Things" (from The Sound of Music)"Red Rain" - Peter Gabriel"Sojourn of Arjuna" - Béla Fleck & the Flecktones"Spain" - Chick Corea"Take Me Out to the Ball Game""What You Won't Do for Love" - Bobby Caldwell"When You Wish Upon a Star" (from Pinnochio)"You Can't Rollerskate in a Buffalo Herd" - Roger MillerTheme from New York, New York - Liza Minnelli/Frank SinatraTheme from The Six Million Dollar ManStudio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

title: Crash Into Me
notes: Crash Into Me was written during the Crash recording sessions in '95. It briefly peeped its head out during a Dave and Tim live soundcheck during a break from the studio. Along with Too Much, this song was played live before the album release date in April of 96. After the album release the song became immensely popular with fans and has enjoyed frequent playing since. Starting on the 2001 Summer Tour the song surprisingly became a rarity, but it has since returned to regular rotation. The "Dixie Chicken" interpolation is also very popular with fans, and is included in the song 90% of the time.In the 2018 "Producers Roundtable" promotional video, John Alagia mentions that an earlier version of this song was titled "Soldier."

title: Dancing Nancies
notes: This simple yet profound number featuring a violin-led climax has been around since the beginning. The now-familiar "Could I have been..." intro developed in early performances, with the first known appearance at 12.9.92, then developing to the 4 current standard lines on 4.2.93 before being arranged into the current order a week later and finally settling into the exact words of their final form on 7.22.93.  An extended spoken intro involving the words "Don't you ever wonder...?" first appeared on 7.20.94.It was recorded for "Under The Table and Dreaming" and was in the top five most played songs consecutively from the summer of 1994 all the way through to 1996. Since then, it has steadly dropped off in number of appearances each tour, only to appear twice in 1999, and once in 2001. Demand by the crowd for the re-establishment of the tune was met during the Summer 2002 tour as it again became a staple of the set.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitarJohn Alagia - vocalsMichael McDonald - vocalsAndrew Page - vocalsJeff Thomas - vocals

title: Seek Up
notes: One of the most common openers from 1994-1996, Seek Up is still a fan favorite and one of the band's most popular openers today.  Though the song has essentially settled into fixed lyrics, it went through some variations in the early days.  In particular one prominent lyric heard in the Remember Two Things version, "Intentions are not wicked, don't be tricked into thinking so", made its last known appearance on 3.27.95.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

title: Bartender
notes: This song of epic proportions originally came into the light as "Reconcile our Differences" / "On Bended Knee" on the 1999 Dave and Tim winter tour. Many claim the song sounded eerily like Leave Me Praying and possibly co-evolved from the same song as Don't Drink the Water. Reconcile Our Differences went through several stages of lyrical evolutions after finally being realized as Bartender by the end of the tour. However, it wasn't until the Summer of 2000 that the song finally came into its own after being reworked in the Lillywhite Studio Sessions into a climactic masterpiece.  Times for this song are far from an exact science.  When it is played out of Big Eyed Fish, song time starts as soon as Boyd's droning intro becomes the most prominent thing you hear.

title: Spotlight
notes: Spotlight was played fairly often in the band's early years but will likely never be played again. Dave has several times expressed his dislike of the song, saying that if it were something other than a song, it would be a turd.On 2.27.93, Dave says that record companies always thought they were "fucking insane," so they wrote this song specifically for the record companies (but that they still thought the band was "fucking insane"). The implication here is that the song was written as an (unsuccessful) effort to get a record deal, which might explain Dave's distaste for the song.

title: Get in Line
notes: This forgotten song made its claim to fame during the 1994 Fall tour as a new song that the band played regularly with alternating lyrics. It was played last on New Year's Eve 1994 and never again made a return despite attempts by fans to remind Dave of the song. It was recorded in the "Crash" sessions, but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998).The song was rumored to have been soundchecked in 1999 to the point where many fans were almost sure of its return. However, it never did and has yet to resurface.

notes: This Daniel Lanois cover made its full-band debut in the summer of 1997 as feature cover song during the tour. It was again played sparsely in 1998 and 1999 with only one appearance in 2000. The song returned again in 2002 as a Dave solo feature.

title: Me and Julio Down by the Schoolyard
sortTitle: me and julio down by the schoolyard
notes: This song, from Paul Simon's eponymous 1972 album, was an early cover that was dropped from the band's catalog once it had been filled with more originals. It was likely played in 1991 and 1992 more than the handful of known performances listed below.The song made a surprising comeback at the 2001 Jazzfest show, with Paul Simon taking Dave's place on guitar and vocals. It has been performed just one other time since, at a Dave solo show in 2014, with Simon as a guest.

title: Angel
notes: Angel sounds like it might have evolved from an intro Dave has done frequently to Jimi Thing. It debuted on 3.19.01 during a Dave solo radio promotional tour for Everyday. It was played most stops on the 2001 Summer Tour with The Lovely Ladies contributing a gospel choir style backing vocal to the main song, then starting 6.4.01 they lead an extended jam where they introduced the band members.DMB subsequently played a regular-length Angel three times early on the 2003 Summer Tour, and the songs hasn't been played or referenced since.

title: Exodus
notes: The band has been covering this Bob Marley tune since the very beginning. It has always been a rarity each tour and was never played with any consistency (that we know of, at least; there are MANY missing setlists from 1991-93). After being played only once during 1996, the song disappeared completely only to come back with a shockingly powerful return on 12.13.00 featuring Warren Haynes. The song almost made a return on 8.25.01, but was only teased.  The band's long anticipated return to Red Rocks became a reality in September 2005.  With an additional 4th show announced as a Hurricane Katrina Benefit, this song came back with a montage of guests.

notes: This song debuted in early 1995 as "40 Always," a calm and mellow love song about devotion. After a string of performances in the Spring Tour of 1995, the song disappeared altogether for 3 years. In the summer of 1998, fans began bringing signs of #40 to shows and handing Dave tapes of the definitive Yoshi's performance. That fall, the song started being teased; finally to resurface in 1999 as a Dave solo feature.The song has made a lot of appearances since summer 1999 to various extents.  We require a partial version to at least go through the part of the song that usually has the word "always" (though occasionally other lyrics are sung during this part).  A tease is anything short of that mark, and a full version is at least two verses and two choruses.

title: Money (That's What I Want)
notes: This song was originally recorded and released as a single by Barrett Strong. It has been covered by many artists,but the Beatles' version is the most popular.  They recorded it and released it in 1963 on their second album With the Beatles.As of 2012 it has only been played in tandem with a cover of Pink Floyd's Money, debuting on 5.31.08.

title: Hey Hey, My My (Into the Black)
sortTitle: hey hey, my my (into the black)
notes: This song was originally released on Neil Young's 1979 partial live album Rust Never Sleeps. It was first covered by Dave at a Neil Young tribute show in 2001, then not played again in any form until the 2008 Summer Tour. In these later versions, Dave often swapped out "rust never sleeps" for "than fade away," the latter of which are lyrics from "My My, Hey Hey (Out of the Blue)" on the same album.As of 2009, this song has always been played without any horns.

title: Grey Street
notes: This feature song made its debut in 2000 as a frequently played feature with alternating lyrics. In fact, it was the most played song of the 2000 summer tour at 46 of 52 shows.In 2001, this song took a back seat to the Everyday songs yet was still one of the top 5 openers of the tour.After its release on Busted Stuff, the extra chorus at the end omitted, the song came back with a vengeance in 2002 with static lyrics and went on to be the second most played song three tours in a row.  On 12.17.02, the song was played with the first chorus and second verse omitted, likely as a trial run for fitting the song into the time slot for the following night's performance on Late Night with Conan O'Brien. On 12.21.02, the arrangement was switched to 1st verse, 2nd chorus, 2nd verse, 3rd chorus, and it was played that way on all performances for over a year. Starting in the Summer 2004 tour, the song began to be performed in the following form: 1st verse, 1st chorus, 2nd verse, 3rd chorus. This is known as the truncated version. Unless otherwise noted, all performances from 12.17.02 through the end of 2019 are truncated version, while all versions from 2020 on are full versions.While some have speculated that this song is about Dave's ex-girlfriend Julia Grey, there is no evidence for this connection. In fact, it's not even known for sure whether Ms. Grey's last name is spelled that way or as Gray. It seems unlikely that Dave would write a song about an ex-girlfriend seven years after breaking up with her and while engaged to his future wife, and there is nothing in the lyrics that lends any credence to the theory that it is about her aside from the mentions of the color gray (which Dave, having been educated in South Africa, spells the British English way).

title: Don't Drink the Water
notes: This famous single was produced in the Studio Sessions for "Before These Crowded Streets" from an effort to entirely rework the raw song, "Leave Me Praying." The result was a politically charged song that became the first single from the new album. From the debuting tour of Spring '98 to the Summer of 99, it was the most frequently played song on every tour including Dave and Tim. In the Spring of 2002, it highlighted as one of the top openers for the tour.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - electric guitar/mandolinBéla Fleck - banjoAlanis Morissette - vocals

title: Lie In Our Graves
notes: Lie In Our Graves is one of the band's the oldest songs, first appearing in early 1992.  It features Dave’s “carpe diem” message, and as Dave has mentioned several times, encourages listeners to have no regrets after dying.  By the end of 1992, a slight Carter solo developed and through the first few shows of 1993, the format which eventually became the staple for the song was established, with LeRoi taking a solo between the main body and the reprise.  Although the extended bridge originally featured LeRoi, after the Crash sessions Boyd took over the solo spot on 4.30.1996 (Crash release show).  The last show to feature a LeRoi solo was 12.5.1995.  After being included on the Crash album, this fan favorite was played at each show on the Fall and New Year’s Eve Run Tours.  It is also currently in the top ten in all time plays after being in heavy rotation all the way up to 2001 and still frequents a fair of amount sets since then.Lie In Our Graves’s structure also allows for a variety of guests.  The elongated bridge section has featured artists such as Big Voice Jack (6.5.1998), David Hidalgo (7.6.1997), Trey Anastasio (5.19.2001) and Butch Taylor on keys.  Preceding the reprise on several versions in 2000 (starting on the West Coast run and including 8.29.2000 – Live Trax 11), Dave takes a moment to refer to Butch as “number 6.”  After a handful of appearances on it in 1996, Béla Fleck emerged as possibly the premiere guest soloist on the song during the 1997 Tour, and has been featured many times since.  Of special note, on 11.21.1998 Boyd and Béla both took solos for the first time.  On the Crash album, Tim Reynolds was featured in the solo spot, and a game of table tennis can be heard in the background. Over the years, Lie In Our Graves has seen many changes. title: Crush
notes: This wildly popular love song from "Before These Crowded Streets" was part of the new onslaught of songs recorded during those studio sessions and quickly evolved an extended jam in the 98 tours and has welcomed guests on it ever since.Dave has said that he lifted the first word from Willie Nelson's Crazy.

title: All Along the Watchtower
notes: "Watchtower" is one of the all time most played encore songs. The band is thought to have been covering this song since at least their third show ever. Dave was known to have been covering it solo since before the band came together. The song became an early favorite; at many older shows, the crowd would scream "Watchtower" throughout the entire show. Watchtower is one of the most guested songs and features many popular jams. It sounds hardly anything like Bob Dylan's original nor the more well-known Jimi Hendrix cover. The song even has evolved over the years, dropping a verse, gaining a Stefan solo feature intro, and a high note during "the wind began to howl." Due to the chord progression's similarity to the ending section of Led Zeppelin's "Stairway to Heaven," DMB's performances of this song have occasionally featured lyrical and/or musical references to Stairway, most notably on 12.1.98 and on the vast majority of performances from 2008–2009 and 2018–present."All Along the Watchtower" originally appears on Bob Dylan's 1967 album John Wesley Harding. Jimi Hendrix's cover appears on the Jimi Hendrix Experience's 1968 album Electric Ladyland, as well as on just about every Hendrix compilation.

title: Typical Situation
notes: Originally, Typical Situation was one of the few core songs from Dave Matthews' early solo repertoire, inspired from by the poem "Prayer in the Pentagon", by RobertDederick (see lyrics). When the full band formed, the song served as one of their first developments of musical collaboration and quickly became a feature performance at shows. During its early period (1992-1993), the song featured an instrumental improv intro, a stop-time breakdown, and frequently slotted as opener, closer, and encore. The song was also a versatile candidate for segues, especially in the famous early triple segue of Minarets > Blue Water > Typical Situation. Eventually replacing Blue Water in the segue, Minarets > Typical Situation became one of the great 1-2 combos of the early years. In early 1994, Dave regularly began evolving an optional short vocal intro. While the exact words occasionally alternated, the theme eventually solidified into: "She stands/stares alone,And a tear fall from her chin...She wonders, after all these years.All this time, all this work to find it...All comes down to nothing..."In 1997, with Bela Fleck guesting on the song, the stop time intro was extended into an elaborate alternate riff that became a crowd favorite. During the 99 Dave and Tim tour, Dave mentions how someone had pointed out to him that there are indeed four oceans, not seven. Dave went on to explain that  what he meant was the seven seas of the ancient world, but "oceans" sounded better. A couple of years later, he would go on to alternate this line into "Seven seas pummel, the shores of you and me", and eventually settled on "Seven days it took to make the world, if you believe".It was confirmed by Stefan to be the opener of the first ever full band show on 3.14.91.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitarSteve Forman - percussion

title: Lover Lay Down
notes: According to a 2014 Jambands.com interview with Boyd, he associates this song with LeRoi, and he had to leave stage the first time they played it after LeRoi's death (8.22.08).The original title, "Paul Simon," comes from the Paul Simon song "50 Ways to Leave Your Lover" and the similarities between the two songs' drum beats.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitar

title: True Reflections
notes: An original Boyd Tinsley song that was arranged into a full band number and received its heaviest playing in 1993, showcasing the band's all-round energy. It was recorded in the "Crash" sessions, but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998). The song enjoyed a comeback in 2000-2001 before being recorded as the title track on Boyd Tinsley's debut solo album, True Reflections, where Dave Matthews lends backing vocals.

title: Cry Freedom
notes: This powerful ballad was around since the very beginning where it featured Dave Solo to start out and then the band joining in. It was finally recorded in the studio and released on the "Crash" album; heavily produced and electrified. Its most popular runs were the fall of 1994, the encore features on the Dave and Tim '96 tour. After 1996, the song dropped off into a rarity and was the rarest song on the Summer '97 tour. After being completely missing in action in 1998, the song experienced a small revival in '99, but then dropped off in 2000 and 2001. However, as teases in the Spring 2002 tour showed, the song was surely not dead.  It returned as a Dave solo in the Summer Tour, and full on in 2003.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

title: Minarets
notes: The studio version on Remember Two Things was recorded at Flat Five Studios in Salem, VA.  This song famously features a live "Scat" intro where Dave scats along to his muted guitar strumming before starting the main riff of the song.On versions from late 1993 to 1995, Carter would also scat along with Dave in the intro. Below are notes about the "Slow and Low" intro:During 1994, Carter interpolated lyrics from the Run-DMC song "Slow and Low" (better known as recorded by the Beastie Boys) during the scat. Here are the lyrics Carter covers:"I do not sing, but I make a songYou'll live your whole life, and I hope you'll live longOn the Gong Show we won't get gongedIt's Run-DMC, not Cheech and Chong"He says it very scattishly, and sometimes it's hard to make out. He also says, "It's (fuckin') DMB", instead of "It's Run-DMC". After this he continues the scat with Dave, often saying something like, "I'm talkin' about Dave...I'm talkin' about Dave", until the main riff hits.It is known that Carter started doing it sometime in early '94 (verified on 1.3.94) and stopped doing it sometime in late '94 (verified on 11.14.94) until he started it again in 2014.Studio personnel:Greg Howard - Chapman stick/synthesizer/percussion samplesTim Reynolds - guitar

title: Blue Water
notes: Blue Water (also known as "Blue Water Baboon Farm") made its greatest contribution early on in 92-93. The song featured a 180 degree mood change to live shows and displayed a more serious and experimental band to the audience. It formed a frequent 1-2 combo with Minarets, but was eventually replaced in the combo by Typical Situation. After the fall of 1993 the song all but disappeared from sets, making brief comeback appearances on 2.25.95 and on the Dave and Tim tours of 1996 and 1997. After having been assumed dead, the song made a surprising comeback on the Fall 2000 Tour as a Dave solo feature. It made an even bigger comeback on the Spring 2009 Tour as a full-band feature, with more and more of the song being played each night before suddenly disappearing again.In July 2007, Stefan posted a thread on izstyle.com asking what song DMB fans missed the most.  The overwhelming winner was Blue Water. He posted: "I loved the responses and am with you on Blue Water.... One of my all time favorites. The solo section bass line from that song was inspired from listening to Pink Floyd all the time when I was 18. The lyrics...beautifully dark...we were a young band when that song was written......Look what you all have done to me, I'm feeling a bit melancholy at the moment :-)Till Tour"For the purposes of distinguishing between full and partial versions of this song, we define a full version as one that contains the lyrics and a jam/solo section. Partial versions have typically omitted the jam/solo section. Teases generally end before the "take this my arms" verse. [NOTE: Previously, we defined a full version as having a return to the lyrics at the end; however, we discovered that not all of the song's original performances had this part, so we revised our criteria.]This song has been officially released twice with printed copyright information (Warehouse 8 Vol. 1 and Live Trax 20), and both instances give it a copyright date of 1990, which seems inaccurate.

title: I'll Back You Up
notes: Before this song was performed during a Dave & Tim performance on 9.24.95, Dave mentions that the song was recorded in an upstairs bathroom (presumeably for Remember Two Things).  Dave jokes that the take that was taken from that Dave & Tim recording was the one with no toilet flushing.  At yet another D&T performance on 2.5.96, Dave mentions this song was written about a girl named Julia.

title: One Sweet World
notes: This is one of Dave's earliest songs and has been played by the full band each and every year since the very beginning. Originally, the song would traditionally feature an instrumental intro before the main riff that was rarely left out. Throughout its evolution, Leroi's horn arrangement was modified during the song's recording on "Remember Two Things" and was played as such until the original horn routine was restored when the song was re-worked for the 2004 Summer Tour. This re-working also involved the band dropping the classic "Instrumental intro" to the song. Instead, Dave would lead the song off with the other band members joining in behind him.A studio recording of Dave playing this song plays during the credits of the 2003 film Go Further. This version was recorded during the Some Devil sessions.The song also features a live outro dubbed "Swim Naked." During the early years, Dave would scat and elaborate the lyrics, but eventually they settled into the following form: Don't you love you swim nakedDon't you love you swim nakedDon't you love you swim naked'Cause I do.

title: Help Myself
notes: Dave took a while to settle on a name for this song, introducing its debut performance as "Help Myself" and later early performances as "Dangerous Hours" or  sometimes "Desperate Hours," before settling on its original title. It was recorded in the "Crash" sessions but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998).Originally they were to have given Halloween for the soundtrack of Scream 2 during the "Before These Crowded Streets" sessions. However, once it was finished, they decided this version of Halloween was too strong, recorded it with the Kronos Quartet and kept it for the album. Then they re-recorded Help Myself and gave it to the movie and soundtrack.

title: Once on a Wild Afternoon
notes: Sometimes called "Destiny," there are only three known complete performances of this song. It likely came from a soundcheck jam done before the 1992 Van Riper's Festival show.

title: Drive In Drive Out
notes: This intense number was there from the very beginning when it was a Dave solo feature for the early days. Much like Tripping Billies was the first dmb song to feature Boyd Tinsley, DIDO was the first song to feature the prowess of Carter Beauford as a full band song. The famous soundcheck of 8.4.92 features the band joyfully shelling it out into a steady feature of 92-93. After only being played once in 1994, the song came in strong in 1995 and peaked in 1996 as one of the most played songs, including one of the top openers AND one of the top closers. Since then, the song enjoyed steady play but has become a rarity since 2005.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

Since then, the song enjoyed steady play but has become a rarity since 2005.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - guitar

title: Tangerine
notes: This ballad was originally released on Led Zeppelin III in 1970. It was one of the last songs by Jimmy Page only recorded by Led Zeppelin. Led Zeppelin played it in acoustic sets throughout 1971 & 1972 and finally at each night of Earl's Court run in London in 1975. Robert Plant introduced it the final night (5.25.75) by describing it as "a song of love. It's innocent and it's especially for... first loves." He also remarked that it was the first song they'd attempted four-part harmonies. It was also played at many shows on Page & Plant tours in the mid to late '90s. There are no official Zeppelin live releases of the song; however, the Earl's Court shows have widely distributed bootleg soundboard and video recordings.Dave played this song on occasion in 1992 and a few times in 1993 as a solo feature opening a set before the band joined in. The first known performance was 7.21.92, but it is highly unlikely that is the actual debut. With as many setlists that haven't survived from that era and as infrequently as it was played, it could've been played several times that aren't known. There's no dialogue about the 7.21 version that would confirm or deny anything. It was brought back for the last two nights of the 1996 Dave & Tim tour but hasn't been mentioned since then. Whenever it has been played, it hasn't included the ending jam from the original version.

title: Granny
notes: This song is one of (if not THE) most popular regularly played songs of all time. If you look at the By Year charts, you'll see that it was played most in 1993 where it was one of the top 3 most played songs through every consecutive tour for that year. All this promotion was probably due to the fact that Granny was supposed to be the band's first 'single'. Matthews admitted that the band envisioned people walking the streets chanting "LOVE! BABY!", and for a while the crowd did chant at several shows during other songs in the setlist. Although it was confirmed to be the only unreleased song recorded for Under The Table and Dreaming (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998), it never quite made it to any other studio albums and is now known for is steady occasional playing. The studio cut was issued on the 2014 20th anniversary release of Under the Table and Dreaming

title: Christmas Song
notes: This quiet tune started out as a Dave Solo feature in early shows, and was included in Remember Two Things as one of the Dave and Tim tracks on the album compilation. Throughout the years, this song has regularly been played during the the pre-holiday season, paying ode to the story of a man who had a profound impact on humanity. This song has always featured Carter on backing vocals and was recorded during the Everyday studio recording sessions for release on the Platinum Christmas compilation disc. During live play, Dave usually quotes a line from The Beatles' "All You Need is Love" and "Can't Buy Me Love".When introducing the song, Dave often gives a disclaimer that the song is "not a religious song, just a story" (09.18.10).The song's debut (introduced that night as "Blood of Our Children") was long thought to be 12.23.92.  When the unknown 12.21.92 show was released by management on June 8, 2010 as part of the DMBlive series, Christmas Song appeared in the encore, moving its known debut date back two days.  There is no dialogue before or after the song to mark its first time being played, so it is still possible that the real debut came earlier than this even.

notes: This song made a brief span of appearances in early 1993. It was inspired by and written for Miguel Valdez, a percussionist who sat in with the band in 1992 and who passed away in early 1993. It was finally released in 1994 on Under the Table and Dreaming (track #34) as an instrumental track. The song is co-written by Haines Fullerton, an early collaborator who took his own life in September 1996. As of 2014, this is the only song to drop out of rotation before its official studio release: by the time Under the Table came out, #34 hadn't been played live in a year and a half and wouldn't be played again for almost eleven more years.The song made a surprising comeback on 7.9.05 after a few teases earlier in the tour. This is currently the longest comeback in DMB history, at 4491 days between performances, or a little over 12 years.  It was played again with some lyrics (for the first time since 1993) on 10.2.07 to celebrate Dave's wife's 34th birthday; it then disappeared again until returning in 2013 for a tease, two partials, and two full performances (one of which was on LeRoi's birthday). It was again played on LeRoi's birthday in 2018.Because this has become an instrumental song, it is difficult to objectively distinguish between teases and partial performances. We consider a performance partial if it has the main riff at least four times (constituting a "verse") followed by the "we'll be okay" part twice (constituting a "chorus").The song was the subject of a clue on the July 12, 2012 episode of "Jeopardy!"Category: 30s RockClue: Track 34 on this band's "Under the Table and Dreaming" is cleverly titled #34.Studio personnel:Tim Reynolds - acoustic guitar

notes: #36 started out as a jammy song in 1993. It was inspired as a result of the tragic assassination of a South African freedom fighter, Chris Hani, and was played for the first time three days after his death in 1993. After serving as a tribute song for almost all of 1994, the song eventually evolved into an inspiration jam about freedom featuring scatting by Dave and a monumental drum solo intro by Carter. Dave noted on 10.9.94 that he changed the lyrics from "Hani, Hani" to "honey, honey," to fit more with the song's mood (though the familiar "come and dance with me" chorus had taken shape by late 1993).1995 through 1996 marked the golden years of this song, where it enjoyed frequent highlights and dancing on the part of the fans.  It was recorded in the Crash sessions, but didn't make the final cut (Steve Lillywhite, Oct. 1998). In 1999 and 2000, the song returned in a new, slower form featuring the Lovely Ladies and without the Carter solo.During the Everyday studio sessions, the song underwent a huge transformation to become the song Everyday. Since then, the spirit of #36 has resurfaced within Everyday in the following forms: 1) Dave sings "Honey Honey" in the intro/outro. 2) Boyd plays the #36 "Honey Honey" melody in the intro/outro3) The song ends in the same fashion as #36 did4) Everyday directly segues into a #36 reprise consisting of a verse and chorus from #36 (e.g. 5.26.01).Here is the original Reuters announcement of Hani's death:April 10, 1993, Saturday, BC cycle AFRICAN NATIONAL CONGRESS OFFICIAL IS ASSASSINATED JOHANNESBURG, SOUTH AFRICA   A senior official of the African National Congress was assassinated on Saturday, the South African Press Association reported. title: Any Noise
notes: This predecessor of Say Goodbye began appearing as periodic Dave solo features during the summer and early fall of 1993, when Dave used to frequently begin each show with several solo performances. During its early days the song's name was disputed by varying perceptions of whether Dave was singing "any noise" or "anti-noise," so it became common to simply write both as the title. According to our lyrical transcribers, he is clearly singing "any noise," but the moniker has stuck. It's worth noting that while it's become standard to put both spellings in writing, it's only necessary to say one of them, as the discrepancy comes from the fact that they are pronounced the same way.It could be that Dave always knew the song as "Say Goodbye" given the fact that he introduced it as such on 9.1.93. However, some elements of the song remained separate from Say Goodbye and actually made a brief appearance in 1995 as a partial song during one of Dave's solo encore performances.

title: Route 2
notes: This song was played once in 1993 completely impromptu due to the fact that the band got lost getting to the venue and showed up late to the gig. As a result, they made up this song on the spot in order to check their sound system.The chord rhythms, vocals, and song structure seem very similar to the song known as 'Proudest Monkey' and so theory has it that 'Proudest Monkey' (which didn't appear until a year later in 1994) is an evolution of this song. title: Nature
notes: The song "Nature" is a Samples cover, released for the first time in May of 1989 as the last track on their self-titled album.  Also known as "The Blue Album," the record was recorded in 1989 and released that year independently by the band. The album caught the attention of executives at Arista records, and the Samples signed their first record contract. The Blue Album was re-released on the Arista label, but the Samples left the label before recording another album, citing irreconcilable differences between the band and the label. The album was released for a third time in 1991 when the band signed with fledgling What Are Records?Dave used to sing some lyrics from "Nature" before Tripping Billies overtop of some chords based around Tripping Billies. For this reason, this intro is known as the "Nature intro" even though it is instrumental now and therefore has no connection to the Samples song.When the intro is listed as a separate song, it refers to an extended Nature intro whose music is still based around Tripping Billies but which has lyrics from the Samples song.

title: Water Into Wine
notes: This song began in late 1994 as an outro to Recently. It was played very often in 1995 but died off after three performances in 1996. Although most fans assumed this little ditty had been permanently abandoned, it made a shocking return more than eleven years later on 6.7.08. It was still an outro to Recently for the first two performances on the 2008 tour, but nearly all of the others came after Eh Hee (the exceptions being 8.5.08 and 8.7.08, when it was played after Halloween). For most of 2009 and 2010, it came after You & Me. Although this song has long been known as "Water/Wine Jam" by fans, it is listed on live setlists and on Live Trax Vol. 13 as "Water into Wine."This song has gone through many significant changes for such a short tune. It was played in pretty much the same way during 1994 and 1995, except for the 8.20.95, which has a "country" flavor to it. In 1996, the band changed the song's key to a minor one, giving it a darker tone. The first few versions of the song from 2008 were much like the original; however, as the tour progressed, the song began to be more of a full-band effort, occasionally being several minutes long. Dave also began incorporating the refrain from the short-lived Round and Round into the song in 2008.This song has been released with two different copyright dates: Live Trax 13 gives it a date of 1994, while Live Trax 15 has 2008. 1994 is the first year it was played live, while 2008 is the first year it was officially released as its own song.

title: Don't Burn the Pig
notes: This song appeared as a full band number at the famous Yoshi's show in 1995. According to story intros, Dave was supposedly inspired to write the song after watching a nature special in which native tribes burned live pigs to determine judgments. The last time it was played by the full band was on 8.31.96. After that the song disappeared almost entirely with the exception of a few Dave and Tim appearances in 1997.It was not until 1998 during the studio sessions of Before These Crowded Streets where the song was majorly reworked into Pig. Although the phrase, "Don't Burn the Pig" was not included in the album version, it resurfaced in teases of the song in 2001.  When "Pig" returned to the setlists in 2002, it was listed on the road page setlists as "Don't Burn the Pig" but every version has been the modern song.

title: Raven
notes: Raven was originally performed as a segment of improv at the beginning of a Dave solo version of Typical Situation in Cincinnati on 8.20.95. This particular version of Raven was bracketed by early improv fragments of Little Thing before segueing into Typical.After this one show, the song was shelved for over four years until it was built up in the Spring 2000 studio sessions leading to its full song debut on 6.20.00 in Columbus. 2000 versions were usually lyrically based around the idea of waste, often times wasted youth and innocence. Like the rest of the Spring 2000 songs, it was left off of the band's next release, Everyday, and unlike Grey Street, Grace Is Gone, Bartender, etc. it did not return to sets the following tour.In 2002 it made its return to live sets on the opening night of the Spring 2002 tour and a lyrically retooled and glossy version appeared on the band's next album, Busted Stuff. After 2002 the song wasn't played again until opening night of the 2006 Summer Tour at Maryland Heights. Played mostly throughout the early part of that tour then abandoned again, it would not return again until the opening night of the 2009 tour. This time it was played without the opening riff and started directly at the first verse with only Dave and Tim playing until the band joins in half-way through the verse.

title: Deed Is Done
notes: It has been said that this song was inspired from a young female fan, "Emily",  who was terminally stricken with leukemia. As the story goes, her last request was to meet Dave Matthews in person, and after visiting her, he was inspired to write "Deed is Done". The song was recorded in Fall of 1995 for the "Crash" album, and was not a "road-tested" tune. It and three other songs recorded ("Help Myself," "Little Thing," and Boyd Tinsley's "True Reflections") were chosen to not be included in the album's line-up. "Deed is Done" was first played during a Dave and Tim live soundcheck for the 9.24.95 show. Has only been played twice by the full band: here and here. The song took its most popular form as a passionate Dave and Tim song where Tim could add his manic sounds to the mood of the song and where Dave has occasionally quoted lines from The Who's "Pinball Wizard". With the release of Before These Crowded Streets in April of 1998, fans heard the "Deed is Done" riff towards the end of the thirty-second jam following "Pig" on the album.

title: Little Thing
notes: During the 4.30.96 performance of this song, Dave refers to the common story spoken over the intro as occurring in January of 1996.This was released as An' Another Thing on Dave's solo release Some Devil with a string background. Current versions are still labeled as "Little Thing," and this isn't moved to defunct because a.) when played on 1.14.04, the official road page labeled it as Little Thing, b.) there was no structural change with the name change, c.) a post-Some Devil release, Live Trax volume 4 (4.30.96) still had it labeled as "Little Thing," and d.) all post-Some Devil stage setlists for which we have scans list it as "Little Thing."In an interview published in the Wall Street Journal on March 6, 2013, Dave was asked which song of his he secretly loves, and he responded, "Little Thing, which is the only song I have recorded that has no words."The chorus was borrowed for the bridge in Dreams of Our Fathers.Because the structure of this song is so fluid and has no set lyrics, it's difficult to categorize performances into "tease" vs. "partial" vs. "full." For our site's purposes, we call any performance with four or more significant sections (verses or choruses) a "full version," and any performance with fewer than two significant parts a "tease" ("significant" here is still fairly subjective, but it means at least 15 seconds' worth of that part's guitar riff).

title: Watermelon Smilin' on the Vine
notes: This is an old folk song written in 1882 and popularized by Uncle Dave Macon in the 1930s. It is commonly known in the DMB community as "The Watermelon Song."As this is a folk song, it has no "official" lyrics. It was commonly taught in grammar schools and at camps in the 20th century, with many lyrical variations later in the century. Most of these variations are to remove some racially insensitive content that was present in the original versions of the song (e.g., the exaggerated African-American dialect used, the stereotype that black people cannot resist watermelon). It is likely that the lyrics Dave sings are the ones he learned as a child in whatever venue.While most versions typically have more verses than Dave's (and the "apples are sweet" verse is actually a refrain), we do not list his renditions as partials since he always sings it the same way (excepting 3.28.03).

title: Captain
notes: On 6.3.96 at an Agents of Good Roots show, a heavily inebriated Dave was asked to come on stage and play a few tunes on Andrew Winn's guitar. One of those was this song, which fans eventually labeled "Crazy," based on the opening lyric. Crazy debuted as a feature seven months later on the Dave and Tim '97 tour, where it underwent some slight lyrical evolution and seemed to have been fully developed; however, the song was missing from the Before These Crowded Streets album and only appeared once on the Dave and Tim '99 tour, where Dave admitted to having forgotten the title. In years following that tour, it seemed as though the song had been forgotten and would never get a chance at full-band play.That all changed in the recording studio of the 2000 Steve Lillywhite sessions, where the song was arranged for the full band and formally titled "Captain." The song was left out of the 2000 tour, however. After the Lillywhite Sessions leaked in March 2001, the song finally surfaced in the following Summer 2001 tour, first as a quick tease, then as a longer, partial version. It was finally released on Busted Stuff in 2002 with reworked lyrics, and it was played live by the full band for the first time on the day of the album's release.When the band began playing the song in 2002, they added a second bridge and chorus section, which we label the "extended version." Performances without this note have the same arrangement as the album, even if they are longer performances due to a guest.Until 2011, the song had only been played by the band twice since 2002 and not at all since 2004. It has been a rarity since then, finding its way into the rotation at least once every few years.
songHistory: On 6.3.96 at an Agents of Good Roots show, a heavily inebriated Dave was asked to come on stage and play a few tunes on Andrew Winn's guitar. One of those was this song, which fans eventually labeled "Crazy," based on the opening lyric. Crazy debuted as a feature seven months later on the Dave and Tim '97 tour, where it underwent some slight lyrical evolution and seemed to have been fully developed; however, the song was missing from the Before These Crowded Streets album and only appeared once on the Dave and Tim '99 tour, where Dave admitted to having forgotten the title. In years following that tour, it seemed as though the song had been forgotten and would never get a chance at full-band play.That all changed in the recording studio of the 2000 Steve Lillywhite sessions, where the song was arranged for the full band and formally titled "Captain." The song was left out of the 2000 tour, however. After the Lillywhite Sessions leaked in March 2001, the song finally surfaced in the following Summer 2001 tour, first as a quick tease, then as a longer, partial version. It was finally released on Busted Stuff in 2002 with reworked lyrics, and it was played live by the full band for the first time on the day of the album's release.When the band began playing the song in 2002, they added a second bridge and chorus section, which we label the "extended version." Performances without this note have the same arrangement as the album, even if they are longer performances due to a guest.Until 2011, the song had only been played by the band twice since 2002 and not at all since 2004. It has been a rarity since then, finding its way into the rotation at least once every few years.

title: Toy Soldiers
notes: With chord progressions similar to "Crash Into Me", this song was briefly known as the 'new song' during the Summer 1996 tour even though it was only played three times. Occupying a slot in between 'Dancing Nancies' and 'Warehouse', it was thought to have evolved from the Rain Intro of that same tour. However, it is clearly a different song. The title was named by fans/tapers and is taken from the last lyrics of the last version of song, on 6.22.96.An interesting note: The lyrics for 6.21.96 contain eerily revealing references to Dave's personal experiences with his family. [thanks to Chris Oberlin, Christine Baginski, and Patrick Anthony Westgate for their initial research into this song]

title: Anyone Seen the Bridge
notes: This famous segue originated from a jam that was performed after So Much to Say on 10.3.96 with Soul Coughing's M. Doughty. The song had a resemblence to Led Zeppelin's "The Crunge" where Robert Plant says, "Has anyone seen the bridge?"  Somehow, the title stuck. Two days later the band decided to play it as a bridge in between So Much To Say and Too Much. The jam was an instant classic. After making a cameo in Before These Crowded Streets, the song went on to become one of the main highlights featuring the band's energy in 1998. In 2000, the song was involved in the suprising So Much to Say > ASTB > Too Much (fake) > Ants Marching sequence.  In the summer of 2003, ASTB turned into a segue for So Much To Say to go to a number of songs including Rapunzel, Spoon, and Warehouse; however, it has since reverted to nearly always segueing into Too Much, or at least a Too Much fake.Around 2007–08, the band used a "freeze time" transition between So Much to Say and Anyone Seen the Bridge, where they would all freeze in place for a few seconds while Carter counted out a number/tempo through the band's IEM system. They would then play that number of "hits" of the last note of So Much to Say before freezing again and repeating a few times, sometimes pausing for quite a while between rounds. The "freeze time" transition got most creative when, on 7.29.08 and 9.6.08, the band played the "hits" to the rhythm of the Tripping Billies intro. [Note: Although this intro comes before Dave sings the final "little baby" from So Much to Say, we consider it part of Anyone Seen the Bridge in order to be consistent with times when the band played it without having played So Much to Say at all.]

title: Cryptorchid
notes: This surprising cover of the Marylin Manson original made its appearances on the Dave and Tim '97 tour, and it was often mistakenly called "Crypto-Child" and "Crypto Torture" on tape setlists. Dave ranted a few times about Marylin Manson's music and how he respected his difficult artistic expression. His performances only covered the song's refrain.The title is the adjective form of cryptorchidism, which is the condition of missing one or both testicles.The lyrics that Dave sings are also in Manson's song Antichrist Superstar, which Tim has covered; however, the melody and chord progression are clearly those of Cryptorchid.

title: Looking at You
notes: This song has been known as "Looking at You" and was long assumed to be a Dave Matthews original; however, it is actually a VERY loose cover of a song made popular by Scott Fitzgerald & Yvonne Keeley in 1977 and also featured in the movie Babe. While Dave's lyrics are almost completely improvised, the song is obviously based on "If I Had Words."It's not really accurate to call this a cover, but it's also not really an original, and we have to put it in one category or the other.

title: Theme From the Bottom
notes: LeRoi guests on this song and Funky Bitch at Phish's 7.21.97 show at Virginia Beach. The jam linking these two songs features a section with Roi playing two saxophones, Trey on three guitars, Mike on two basses, Fish with many drumsticks, and Page playing with his hands and feet.  Days after LeRoi passed away in 2008, Trey Anastasio described the scene on his webpage:"When Phish played Virginia Beach in 1997, LeRoi came on stage and started playing three saxophones at once and set off the most hilarious chain of events. Within minutes, Page was playing four of his keyboards with his hands and feet. I had three guitars on and drumsticks in my hand banging on Fish's drums, and Mike was playing two basses. It was just a phenomenal moment! And I remember LeRoi laughing with his mouth full of saxophones. We miss you LeRoi and thank you for the joy you brought all of us.- Trey Anastasio""Theme from the Bottom" originally appears on Phish's 1996 album Billy Breathes.
composer: Trey Anastasio, Jonathan Fishman, Mike Gordon, Tom Marshall & Page McConnell

title: Pig
notes: Through the decades after its release, Pig has grown into a song with one of the deepest connections between the band and the fans. It debuted as a drum groove with a simple three-note guitar rhythm called Don't Burn the Pig on 5.10.1995 on the famous Yoshi's radio show. It was only played a handful of times full band in 1995 and 1996, and teased a few times during the Dave & Tim 1997 Tour. When DMB went into the studio for Before These Crowded Streets, they revisited the song - increasing the tempo and adding pre-chorus and chorus sections, changing the lyrics to a message of living and loving for today.Pig got consistent play in the 1998 and 1999, but disappeared in 2000 as DMB rotated in a new set of songs. Ironically when many of those songs were set aside in 2001 (both in the studio and live), the biggest fan outcry was for the return of Pig. Fans began bringing signs to shows for the song, throwing stuffed pigs on stage, and handing out mass-printed paper. This led to a sometimes playful teasing of fans and the song throughout the tour - most famously at Soldier Field 7.7.01 when Dave teased it 6 times throughout the show.When DMB opened their Spring 2002 tour on 4.4.02 they surprised the crowd by opening the encore with Pig, and to date 2002 saw the most plays of any year by a wide margin. While Dave & Tim casually played Don't Burn the Pig a few times in 1996 and 1997, they didn't play Pig itself until 6.3.17.Versions from 2002 on have sometimes been officially referred to on setlist and releases as "Don't Burn the Pig" due to Dave singing that line instead of "don't burn the day" (and sometimes even with the standard album lyrics). These versions are not the same as Pig's earlier predecessor of the same name, though, and all are called "Pig" on this site.

title: LeRoi solo
notes: The "LeRoi solo" is the beginnings of the Heartbeat intro.  It started early in the Spring 1998 tour as Dave played around with the chords to Pantala Naga Pampa, eventually LeRoi began soloing over it.  This evolved into a bit more of a structed jam with Stefan and Carter adding the Heartbeat with the bass drum in the Summer of 1998.  Note - There still might be a couple instances of the Heartbeat being listed as LeRoi solo or the other way around.  It is on the list of things to be double checked.  When this message is removed, everything is guaranteed accurate.

title: Back to Alexandra
notes: This is song by Big Voice Jack, a legendary South African pennywhistle player which he wrote to give vent to his lifelong hatred for guns. How he came into contact with DMB is an interesting story. He was playing at a club in South Africa when Dave was in the audience. Roi had asked Dave to pick up a few pennywhistles while he was visiting South Africa and Dave approached Big Voice Jack after the gig to ask him where he could find them. Big Voice Jack decided to rather give his own whistles to Dave. "I thought that I would never play in a big stadium in America, so I wanted my whistles to be there," says Jack. "So I gave them to him." Jack finally did get to play in a stadium when he guested with DMB on this song on 6.7.98.In the summer of 1998, the band repaid his generosity by inviting him to come to the States and play with them on the big stadium stages.In the Back to Alexandra documentary, Dave mentions that the band had heard this song on a CD that Lerole had made of his band's songs, but that CD doesn't appear to have ever been officially released.Alexandra is a town in South Africa near Johannesburg and is Lerole's hometown.

title: Memory Motel
notes: Dave sings backup on this song on the Rolling Stones' 1998 album No Security. Dave performed this song with the Stones five times on their 1998 tour of Europe, and the version on this record is taken from the July 5 performance.Dave has recounted that he was in the bathtub in Amsterdam when he got a call from Mick Jagger about guesting with them on this song, which he had never heard before. He pretended that he knew the song and then had to find a copy of the Black and Blue album to learn it. On the first night, the Teleprompter didn't advance, so it just said "Memory Motel" for the duration of the song.

title: Rain Down on Me
notes: This is the song that was refined into the more well-known JTR, and for many years was called John the Revelator.There is evidence of the riff originating in 1998, but the song itself was hashed out during Dave Matthews' collaboration with Carlos Santana in the studio sessions for the album 'Supernatural' released in 1999.  While this song never made it onto the album proper, it was included on the "Legacy Edition" of Supernatural, released in 2010, finally establishing its official title (it's likely, though, that this title was used only to avoid confusion with the traditional song called "John the Revelator," which is interpolated in this song but is not the same song).  It also surfaced live during a few Dave and Tim and full band shows in 1999, 4 out of 5 of which Carlos Santana guested on.During the Lillywhite studio sessions prior to the summer tour of 2000, the "John the Revelator" lyrics were removed and the title was shortened to "JTR." While the riff of the song is original, the "John the Revelator" refrain comes from a traditional gospel song.

While this song never made it onto the album proper, it was included on the "Legacy Edition" of Supernatural, released in 2010, finally establishing its official title (it's likely, though, that this title was used only to avoid confusion with the traditional song called "John the Revelator," which is interpolated in this song but is not the same song).  It also surfaced live during a few Dave and Tim and full band shows in 1999, 4 out of 5 of which Carlos Santana guested on.During the Lillywhite studio sessions prior to the summer tour of 2000, the "John the Revelator" lyrics were removed and the title was shortened to "JTR." While the riff of the song is original, the "John the Revelator" refrain comes from a traditional gospel song.

title: Busted Stuff
notes: This jazzy number was the last Lillywhite Sessions song to debut on the Summer 2000 Tour featuring Butch Taylor and the Lovely Ladies. Dave introduced it as "She My Bitch" at its first few performances (this was likely never a serious title but rather an early, improvised studio lyric from the "she my one" bridge).The song turned up missing in 2001 but made a return in early 2002. About two months before the Busted Stuff album was released, however, it again went missing for about eight years aside from two Dave & Tim performances in 2003, and it hadn't been played since 2010 until it once again returned in 2023.

title: Everyday
notes: This famous song evolved from #36 during the studio sessions for Everyday. Its lyrics partially appeared live as an interpolation in #41 during the Fall 2000 tour. During its first live appearances in the Summer 2001 tour, the song almost immediately drew from its #36 influences; specifically the "Honey, Honey / Come and dance with me" lines. On 5.26.01, the audience shocked the band by chanting the same lines during the show's closer and throughout the encore break. This historic event started a trend of audience participation in the song. Throughout the rest of 2001 and 2002, Dave began resurrecting scatting as well as old lines from #36 developing what is known as the #36 reprise. During Fall of 2002, the audience chants became commonplace as the song was one of the top 5 songs to appear in the encore.

title: When the World Ends
notes: "When the World Ends" was to be the third single from Everyday, but it was replaced by the album's title track after the September 11th terrorist attacks. A video was filmed for the song but never saw release.The song is perhaps most well known for its abrupt ending, ironically during the line, "We'll just be begi[nning]." On the 2003 Dave and Tim tour, the song featured an extended outro in lieu of this abrupt cutoff.Dave drew inspiration for this song's lyrics from the song "The Dance" by Code Magenta, LeRoi's side project, whose lyrics repeat the phrase "When the world ends."

title: Waste
notes: Dave recorded this Phish song for the 2001 Phish tribute album Sharin' in the Groove. The album comprises Phish songs played by bands and artists by whom Phish was influenced, and the CD booklet has the following to say about Dave's contribution: "Dave's unique guitar tunings and distinctive voice intrigued Phish early on. His band opened three Phish shows, he shared the stage with them on at least two other occasions, and Trey recently (5-20-01) [ed. note: the correct date is 5.19.01] performed with the Dave Matthews Band. Here Dave offers a rare acoustic release, playing a song from the Phish album he has said brought him near tears." It's unclear what "unique guitar tunings" they're talking about, but it's a nice note nonetheless.Dave frequently played this song to open encores on the 2001 tour. At Dave and Friends shows, Dave and Trey sometimes played this song during their acoustic mini-set during the encore, alternating verses and singing harmony on the refrain. Since 2002, it has only been played without Trey guesting once.

title: Big Eyed Fish
notes: Also known as "One Eyed Fish," this is one of the few songs to have been teased in concert before it was fully played. Having been recorded in the Lillywhite Studio Sessions, Big Eyed Fish was released through a demo leak and became incredibly popular with fans. Known to have been soundchecked in 2000, fans knew the song had a high potential to be played in 2001 and drew up signs for it early on on the 2001 summer tour. And as the teases foreshadowed, the song made its debut in the encore of 5.14.01 as "Fish Monkey Man" on the 2001 Summer Tour. It quickly developed into the band's showpiece as classic 1-2 combo with Bartender featuring Roi on the Pennywhistle bridge. Since then, it has only been played full band a few times without preceding Bartender. When it is played into Bartender, song time ends as soon as Boyd's droning intro becomes the most prominent thing you hear.

title: So Damn Lucky
notes: The following commentary about this song is found in the Some Devil packaging: "One dead still hot summer's day I was sitting with some friends and family by a great old leaf-covered box elder in my garden. We were wondering when it might finally give up and collapse under the weight of its heavy, heavy limbs. And then, as if it were listening in on our conversation, it creaked, and cracked, and fell down right there in front of us. I'm glad no one was sleeping under it. It did, however, flatten my canoe." On 9.25.22, Dave states that this song is about an accident he had in his Sunbird in the 80s, "if things had not turned out quite so well."Along with The Long Black Veil, Eh Hee, The Maker, Gravedigger, and Stay or Leave, this is one of just six songs that have been played by DMB, Dave & Tim, Dave & Friends, and Dave solo.

title: Some Devil

title: Oh
notes: First verse teased by Dave on 8.26.02.  Chorus teased on the 2003 Dave and Tim tour, both by itself on 3.25.03 and during the Everyday outro on 3.28.03.  It was played in full by Dave and Friends at almost every show of the 2003-2004 Winter Tour and has been a staple of both Dave & Tim and Dave solo shows ever since.Dave (9.23.12): "You know, a lot of people, you fall in and out of love and you fall in love and you find someone and all that... I tell you what, my grandfather and my grandmother (my mom's parents), my mom said when she was little they were so in love that she almost sometimes felt that they didn't even know that they had a couple kids. 'Cause they were crazy in love. And that's sometimes, you know. It doesn't happen to all of us, but you know. But you could almost touch how totally in love my grandparents were. And then even after my grandmother died, it was like this funny echo in the room. And my grandfather, he didn't dawdle, he didn't think about romantic things that often. But boy was he in love. Anyway they were in love and kind of wrote this song about them."

title: Butterfly
notes: First appearing on the Dave and Friends 2003 Winter Tour, Butterfly is a lullaby-like song Dave wrote while filming for “Because of Winn Dixie” and says he, “was thinking about my little girls”(01.16.2004). It was played sparingly until achieving moderate success towards the end of the 2006 Summer Tour as a Dave solo encore opener. Dave encourages listeners to live life despite the sudden and often harsh bumps in the road. Although it does not appear on the film’s soundtrack album, a recording of the song was released on a CD sent to Academy voters for Oscar consideration for Best Original song.

title: Gas Into Fire
notes: This song originated as an interlude with lyrics played prior to 'What Would You Say' on 6.17.04. The riff was also teased several other times later on in the tour. It was originally mislabeled as "Cigarette Lit" before it was identified as a different song altogether. Eventually, it came to be identified as "Gas Into Fire," "Interlude," or "6/17/04 improv" by different sources in the fan community. The song was finally released on the Warehouse 5 vol. 11/Warehouse 8 vol. 8 disc under the title "Gas Into Fire."

title: Crazy Easy
notes: Crazy Easy (sometimes written with a hyphen between the two words) was one of five new songs on the 2004 Summer tour. It was played at nearly every show on that tour and featured a different set of improvised lyrics each night. It was also the first of the 2004 songs to disappear from setlists after not being played at all on the 2004 Vote for Change tour. By the time it was finally played again in 2013, Dave had written lyrics for it and changed his guitar part slightly.

title: Hello Again
notes: An uncommon "story" song by the band, Hello Again is an old-fashioned first person tale of past murder and the mental anguish that follows.  It debuted as one of the "Summer 2004" songs with Crazy-Easy, Sugar Will and Joyride and was referred to as New Song #3 on the dmband.com websites.  It can be assumed that the song was a late addition to the album because the promotional copies sent to Australia to promote the Spring tour there have it added on to the end of the disc with Joyride after the album closer, Hunger For the Great Light.  Trouble With You was in its place in track 9 after Out of My Hands and before Louisiana Bayou.  When the album was finally released several months later, Trouble With You was removed from the album and added to the bonus disc, while a severely and abruptly truncated version of Hello Again took its place.  The first few notes of Louisiana Bayou were eliminated to make the segue even more disjointed.Live, the song developed throughout the 2004 Summer Tour. Initial chord strummings, which Dave often does before songs, evolved into an intro complete with lyrics, which then evolved (most likely) into the song #27, written before the 2007 Summer Tour.  A rare mistake by Carter mid-tour led to an impromptu drum solo in the middle, which was added into all subsequent versions.  After being a staple of 2004 and early 2005 shows, Hello Again has become a bit of a rarity, and took the entire 2006 Summer Tour off.

title: Taps
notes: In July 1862, after the Seven Days battles at Harrison's Landing (near Richmond), Virginia, the wounded Commander of the 3rd Brigade, 1st Division, V Army Corps, Army of the Potomac, General Daniel Butterfield reworked, with his bugler Oliver Wilcox Norton, another bugle call, "Scott Tattoo," to create Taps. He thought that the regular call for Lights Out was too formal. The custom, thus originated, was taken up throughout the Army of the Potomac and finally confirmed by orders." Soon other Union units began using Taps, and even a few Confederate units began using it as well. After the war, Taps became an official bugle call.

title: American Baby Intro
notes: Second to last song off of the Stand Up album to debut live, this song only clocks at 1:57 on the studio album but averaged 7:30 on its debut tour. All time, this song holds the record for highest ratio of album length to average live length (4.29). It is one of the more recent songs in DMB history to feature extensive live instrumental solos (violin, guitar, horn) not present on the album version. Lyrically, although there are few lyrics, a general theme can be derived through the following evidence: 1) War theme: Album version features gunfire/war sounds 2) Matrimony/Lovers theme: "She said... yeah"3) Soldier going off to war: 5.27.07 Alternate Lyrics4) Unwilling distance separation of lovers: 6.7.05 Improv Jam lyrics, 7.27.05 Two Step Intro lyrics.It was played most extensively in the 2005 Summer Tour. title: Idea of You
notes: Illicit and immoral obsession? Fond remembrance of childhood love? The lyrics taken at face value can make you uncomfortable in many versions. Then again, when we think about ex-loves, we remember them as they were, not as they are now. Some versions seem one way, some versions seem the other, but it's clear the person singing isn't 100% OK with his feelings. In 2007, Dave prefaced the song with "this is a song about my babysitter when I was a kid." The lyrics have changed slightly to fall in line.The Idea of You debuted at the former Deer Creek Music Center on June 2, 2006. It also marked the debut of the Veillette soprano 12-string (originally used exclusively on this and Shotgun), and was the first full band song to be played from the 2006 Spring studio sessions. Of all the 2006 songs, The Idea of You was the most complete when debuted. Its compact structure and standardized harmonic horn duet leave only the lyrics unfinished (though most contain the theme and several lines are consistent).Many times Dave has said afterwards how much he loves the song and it showed with steady play throughout the 2006 Summer Tour.

title: Shotgun
notes: Shotgun was the third song to debut on the 2006 Summer Tour.  It was one of the first songs that Dave played on his Veillette soprano 12-string (The Idea of You being the first).  Its first two performances were the main riff repeated in Loving Wings and Proudest Monkey style from start to finish.  In Hershey on June 23, 2006, after work in soundchecks, a new intro and outro were played with the core riff remaining to form the meat of the song.  While the performance was uncomfortable and the song just sort of ended, the new potential was obvious.  Shotgun was absent from setlists, but present at more than a handful of a soundchecks until it finally "re-debuted" on August 16, 2006 in Antioch with a new structure employing the intro and outro.  The recording of this version foreshadows the enduring love that the song would garner.  After the new soft intro, when Dave played the main riff and the rest of the band came in, the audience popped large with more appreciation than most fairly unheard originals receive.  It stayed strong until the end of the tour and several versions also repeated a light reprise of the bridge by Butch and the horns after the song ended.After several starts and stops with new songs previously (most recently when most of the 2004 Summer songs did not return in 2005), Shotgun was brought back on the 2007 tour in fairly regular rotation. title: Sweet Caroline
notes: A catchy tune that peaked at number four in August 1970, the same month it earned a gold record certification for sales of one million singles. (Starting in February 1971, Uni added the track to issues of Brother Love's Travelling Salvation Show, which eventually earned its own gold record certification for sales of 500,000 LPs.) It also became Diamond's first single to place on Billboard's Easy Listening (later Adult Contemporary or AC) chart, where it peaked at number three. Eventually, it earned a platinum record certification for sales of two million singles. (The song was widely covered by other artists. In 1970, Anthony Armstrong took it into the Top 40 of the country charts. In 1972, Bobby Womack took it into the Top 20 of the R&B charts. And it has appeared on chart albums by Andy Williams, Bobby Goldsboro, Elvis Presley, the Ventures, Ray Conniff, Boots Randolph, Frank Sinatra, and Waylon Jennings.) (Thanks to allmusic.com)As it has been a recent Fenway Park tradition to play the song over the PA after the 7th inning, DMB played this for the first time after the seventh song at the 7.8.2006 Fenway Park concert.

title: Nothing From Nothing
notes: This Billy Preston cover was only played one time, at London's Wembley Arena on 5.30.07. The song was originally released on Preston's 1974 album The Kids and Me. title: Falling Off the Roof
notes: This song, played on the Veillette guitar, was played once in 2007 but then seemed to disappear. In 2009, however, Dave and Carter recorded the song with Herbie Hancock for the latter's album The Imagine Project, released on June 22, 2010. While the album's initial press release listed the song (retitled "Jackpot" from the original "Falling Off the Roof") as appearing on the album and promotional videos showed Dave recording it with Hancock, the song ended up being replaced by a cover of the Beatles' Tomorrow Never Knows. According to an interview with Herbie Hancock, Jackpot was dropped because "Dave decided he hated the song."The song was originally written for an unknown movie's soundtrack, but it was not included in whatever film it was.In early 2017, it was registered with ASCAP as "Falling off the Roof" with "Jackpot" listed as an alternate title.

title: Ten Years Gone
notes: Originally released on Led Zeppelin's 1975 double LP "Physical Graffiti," it was covered by Tim as part of a solo mini-Zeppelin medley with Kashmir and his own Stream on 7.28.07 and again with just Kashmir on 6.18.17.It was added to Zeppelin setlists in 1977 when John Paul Jones added an Andy Manson custom Triple Neck (Mandolin, 12 string & 6 string acoustic, complete with bass pedals) to his repertoire.  The song was introduced live by Robert Plant as "a song about your first love, the one you never forget" and is about an ex who made him choose between her and his music.
