title: Rye Whiskey
notes: This song is properly called "Jack of Diamonds," but Tex Ritter's early-1930s version, which is the earliest and most well-known recording, is called "Rye Whiskey."Dave began performing this song shortly before he played at Pete Seeger's 90th birthday party in New York. Dave's rendition of the song is modeled after Seeger's 1954 recording, which is entitled "Whiskey, Rye Whiskey."As this is a traditional song, there are several different titles and many variations in the song itself.We have included the lyrics to Seeger's version of the song as the main lyrics.

title: Beach Ball
notes: Beach Ball was brand new song that was recorded during the Big Whiskey sessions and released on the Little Red Bird bonus disc included with the deluxe edition boxed set.  Nothing was known about the song at all until its debut 4.29.09 mid-set night 2 in Alpharetta, Georgia.  After that performance, a brief interpolation by Jeff and Rashawn was noticed in the 4.15.09 version of Two Step.  It is played on the raised B guitar that is also used for You Never Know, Stay or Leave, Sister, etc.  The version played that night was the same structure and length as the studio version but when it was played again at the next show two days later on 5.1.09 at The Woodlands, it contained an extended section in the middle that included a small, controlled Boyd solo and a duet between Rashawn and Jeff before coming back into the final pre-chorus and chorus.  The version was two and a half minutes longer than the debut and was released as an online-only bonus track on August 11, 2009 to fans who ordered the Big Whiskey and the GrooGrux King: iTunes Pass.  Despite the heavy play of Big Whiskey songs over the next three years, Beach Ball wasn't played again though it was one of the more well-received songs from the sessions.  Signs at a show in Tampa on 7.28.10 prompted Dave to play an impromptu first verse and chorus, accompanied by some light percussion from Carter before it gave way in a segue to Stay or Leave.  Finally, it was played for the first time Dave & Tim on 12.10.10 in Las Vegas.  This version didn't contain the middle jam, though Tim solos replaced the horn lead between verses.  However, the soft instrumental outro from the album was repeated a handful of extra times while Tim soloed over it.  Before this version, Dave said that he liked the song, but didn't play it often and asked for luck.  Afterwards, he said his puppy Angus would destroy his beach ball.

title: Dirty Water
notes: "Dirty Water" is a 12-bar blues song composed by Ed Cobb and first recorded by the California rock and roll band The Standells in 1966. The song is a paean to the city of Boston and its then-famously polluted Boston Harbor and Charles River. The Boston Red Sox baseball team is well-known for playing the song immediately following home victories, a tradition that began in the 2001 season. [from Wikipedia]DMB played this song during the encore of the second night at Fenway Park in 2009.

title: Little Red Bird
notes: Little Red Bird is a song released on the Big Whiskey and the Groogrux King bonus disc, also titled Little Red Bird.  It made its live debut at a Dave & Tim show on 12.10.09 in Las Vegas, the first of three nights.  It was also played 12.12.09 (the third night), marking its live release debut on the Live in Las Vegas full show release.  The song made its debut at a full band show in the encore, though played solo by Dave, on 6.25.10 in Cuyahoga Falls.  At that show, Dave gave the following introduction providing some background into the writing of the song:"I was visiting, a few years ago with a friend of mine, I was visiting these little tiny islands in the middle of the Indian Ocean and there was this little, little bird and it had just one leg.  But it would come and bounce around underneath the chair, and so you knew it was that bird 'cause I'm sure there's just not a bunch of tiny little red birds with one leg. So then I wrote this song over there a couple years ago or something like that."

title: Gotta Travel On
notes: "Gotta Travel On" has its origin in British folk music.  It was adapted by Paul Clayton and recorded in 1958 by Billy Grammer, then released as a single a year later and it sold over a million copies and launched Grammer's career.  It was most famously covered by Bob Dylan for his 1970 album Self Portrait.  It has only been played once and it closed a Dave solo show.  Neil Young sat in as a guest for the performance at the 25th Anniversary Bridge Gala in San Francisco on 10.14.11.  Perhaps not coincidentally, Grammer had passed away just two months earlier at the age of 85.As of early 2012, no recording of the performance is in circulation.

title: Mercy
notes: With only one day's notice, Dave debuted Mercy on the 4.24.12 episode of Late Night with Jimmy Fallon, with the Roots' "Captain" Kirk Douglas accompanying him on acoustic guitar and backing vocals (the Roots are Jimmy Fallon's house band).  Stefan confirmed on twitter that it was a full band song.  Dave played it solo at few benefits in the next two weeks before the Summer Tour started.The full version debuted at the Woodlands tour opener 5.18.12 as part of a three song run of the new album songs.  That performance revealed that Rashawn would handle the harmonies and after starting on the guitar, Dave moved to the piano for an instrumental outro that was not featured on the earlier versions.  Starting at Scranton on 5.28.12, the outro melted in Out of My Hands (with Dave already at the piano).  This combination of songs would continue through the tour with about half of the subsequent versions continuing with the segue.  Like the other songs from the new album, Mercy was played consistently during the tour, but not every night.On June 27th, with the album announcement, management also revealed that Mercy would be the lead single of their upcoming album "Away From the World." A youtube video of the studio version was released that was fairly identical to the live versions, with the addition of strings in the outro. Steve Lillywhite on his twitter account said the radio edit would end at the end of Dave's lyrics and not contain the outro.

title: Gaucho
notes: Originally posted on our front page May 19, 2012:"It's not a groundbreaking notion that information spreads faster each year. Yet over the past six months, very little information has been revealed or leaked about DMB's return to the studio. Any sort of official word has come slowly and infrequently. It was rumored that the band was working with Steve Lillywhite (follow @sillywhite)when he was publicly speaking about his time with DMB and it was clear that he was going through old tapes. Then a series of tweets starting in November 2011 (including pictures of Seattle) made it all but official that right after New Year's they would be back in the studio together. Even after this, however, their collaboration still wasn't confirmed until mid-February by management.Near the end of the sessions, Steve started giving a few updates as to where he was in the post-production process and began answering the occasional questions on twitter.  But aside from that, neither he nor management divulged anything about the tracklisting. The first big reveal came on April 24, 2012 when Dave played "Mercy" solo on Late Night with Jimmy Fallon. But the silence was broken with a blast on May 18, 2012 when the band/ management released the studio version of a new, unheard song called "Gaucho" to celebrate the return of the Summer Tour. Contributing to the surprise was the release of a lengthy video tribute to 2010's tour opener the day before. It was worthy of celebrating the opener by itself because included rare archive video of an unreleased show and the full JTR tour opener.A link to the song (click here) was sent out to DMB's mailing list with a unique code for a free download. title: Take Me to Tomorrow
notes: "Take Me to Tomorrow" is the title track from John Denver's second album, released in 1970. Dave recorded it in the studio for release on the 2013 tribute album The Music Is You.Dave is quoted in an NPR Music article dated April 13, 2013, as saying, "[Denver] was eager to get a different sound, but I thought maybe he didn't serve the lyrics to [Take Me to Tomorrow] as well as he could have.... So I tried a different angle. I don't know if I've managed to serve them any better, but I certainly took a different route than he did."Note: This song's "main lyrics" are transcribed from Dave's studio recording of the song.

title: Kill the Preacher
notes: This song originated in 2012 as an intro to Why I Am with improvised lyrics. Dave again played it before Why I Am on June 1, 2013, but this time he sang from handwritten lyrics, and the official setlist page gave it the title of "Kill the Preacher." After the 7.6.13 show, DMB's official Facebook page began listing it as "Preacher Man" while the dmband.com setlist page continued to list it as "Kill the Preacher." It has yet to appear on any of the printed setlists.Because the 2013 versions have mostly been Dave solo (with some minor accompaniment by Tim and Carter), playing the exact same guitar part as the 2012 versions, we are currently considering all performances to be the same song. However, because the 2012 versions were far more improvised and because the 2013 ones still seem to be works in progress, we are currently looking at the two years separately with regard to whether a performance is a tease, partial, or full. For 2012, we have labeled the relatively lengthy, full-band versions as "full" while calling the medium-length ones "partials" and the shortest ones "teases." For 2013 and on, a full version is one with two refrains, a verse, and a bridge.

title: Death on the High Seas
notes: Dave mentioned this song in a video posted in July 2013. It debuted nearly two years later as an intro to Out of My Hands on 5.30.15 (listed on the official dmband.com setlist as "Intro") before making its full debut on 6.12.15.In the aforementioned video, Dave says of the song: "There's a song that I'm working on now, a little piano tune. What's it called? Um... I don't know what I'm calling it. [hums verse] I think it's called 'Death on the High Seas' or something, which is a very happy-sounding title. But it's sort of all about this sort of overwhelming sense of responsibility that's inside of me, that wants to look after my kids. It's an overwhelming feeling, the feeling of what's entrusted to me by biology. And so it gets a little specific, and it reflects political ideas, and it reflects my fear of how the planet, and it reflects my fear of violence towards children and all those things that my children, my kids will face. And that's sort of what it's about. But I don't think I would have written from such a personal perspective before I had kids. I couldn't have, right?"

title: Again and Again
notes: This song (originally known as "Bob Law"), along with Bismarck and Here On Out, was one of three songs whose titles were leaked from the band's studio sessions in 2015. When played on 5.7.16, Dave said that it didn't have a name yet; however, this title was printed on the setlist. After a few plays, Dave began calling the song "blah blah blah," which the "Bob Law" title seems to be a play on (and also a possible Arrested Development reference). When the tracklist for Come Tomorrow was revealed, Mark Batson confirmed on Instagram that the song had been retitled to "Again and Again."

title: Do You Remember
notes: Dave wrote this song about the loss of a friend shortly before Farm Aid '17 and debuted it at that festival (9.16.17), having just played it for Tim for the first time earlier in the day.Before the 1.12.18 performance, Dave said that the song is "about things that have gone past that we loved, but that's okay, but things that we loved that are no longer with us."He expanded his explanation of the song at various stops on the 2018 Summer Tour with the most complete one so far in Clarkston on 6.6.18:"So uh, I know that, you know, that we all... there's times in our lives when our hearts get broken, and uh, but that's all part of it, you know because that's part of what makes it so beautiful but uh, but uh, a coup- a little while ago I lost a very good friend of mine and we were having the best day of, uh you know one of those best days where the weather's right, the air is right, and the laughter's right, and everything's right. Anyway, I lost my friend on this beautiful day and I suppose my memory of that day is mostly good except for the end of it. But this song kind of explains a little bit about I feel about my missing of my very good friend."

title: Singing From the Windows
notes: Dave first played this song during his solo performance from his house on 3.26.20. His vocal coach, RAab, said that night that its title is "Windows," and it was copyrighted that same day as such. When the song was broadcast on SiriusXM the following week, it was tagged as "Singing From the Windows." The official DMB Facebook page referred to it as "Shadows on the Wall" in a post on April 13. The song has been registered with ASCAP as both "Singing From the Windows" and "Shadows on the Wall."

title: Cha Cha
notes: This song was registered for copyright under the title "Cha Cha" on October 19, 2016. It was first played live on 6.16.23 and was written as "Cha Cha (Only Takes a Moment)" on the handwritten setlist, "Cha Cha" on the printed setlist, and "Only Takes a Moment" on the official band website's setlist. Dave introduced the song as essentially untitled for the first few performances but on 7.11.23 introduced it as "Cha Cha." If/when a version appears on a live release or on the band's YouTube channel, we will have a better idea of its proper title and will adjust accordingly.
